topic: Bermuda Triangle / East Japan geometric symmetry
our_argument_summary: Our review verdicts this as 'Refuted by Data' — NOAA officially states no evidence of anomalous disappearances, Lloyd's does not charge premium rates for the Bermuda region, and U.S. Coast Guard reviews found no unusual causes. The dome claims two agonic line locations at 180-degree symmetry correspond to disappearance zones.

relevant_files: None — WIN-028 is completely absent from monitor.py
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No calculation pipeline exists. WIN-028 is not validated, computed, or monitored by any script in the dome's repository. It appears only as a title line in the wins registry. The monitor.py 39-domain engine does not include any domain for geomagnetic symmetry, agonic lines, or disappearance zone validation.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: The entire WIN is a static assertion — no code computes or validates the claimed symmetry or the disappearance correlation. There is no geomagnetic declination calculation, no agonic line tracking, and no disappearance rate data anywhere in the codebase.
data_sources_used: None — no data is fetched for this WIN
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None needed since no validation is performed
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: Our review does not make specific claims about the dome's code for this WIN, which is appropriate since no code exists for it.

holes_found.description: Our rebuttal is accurate but shallow. We cite NOAA, Lloyd's, and USCG debunking the Bermuda Triangle as a disappearance zone — which is correct — but we don't address the geometric/geomagnetic claim at all. The dome's claim has TWO parts: (1) the regions are disappearance zones, and (2) they show 180-degree longitudinal symmetry related to agonic lines. Our rebuttal only addresses part 1. A defender could say 'even if disappearances aren't anomalous, the agonic line symmetry is still a geometric prediction of the dome.' We should address and refute both halves.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a sentence noting that the two regions are NOT at 180-degree separation (Bermuda ~71W, Devil's Sea ~137E = 208 degrees apart, 28 degrees off from 180°), that the agonic lines do NOT pass through either region precisely, and that agonic line positions are products of the geodynamo (standard model) and migrate over time — they aren't fixed geometric features of any cavity.

holes_found.description: The NOAA link in our evidence text (https://www.noaa.gov/) is generic — it links to NOAA's homepage, not to any specific NOAA statement about the Bermuda Triangle. This is a weak citation. NOAA's actual Bermuda Triangle page would be more convincing.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace the generic NOAA link with NOAA's specific ocean facts page about the Bermuda Triangle, or at minimum cite the specific NOAA statement: 'environmental factors such as hurricanes, sudden weather shifts from the Gulf Stream, and hazardous shallow waters.'

holes_found.description: The detail_evidence text appears to have an incomplete sentence: 'does not charge premium rates for the region.' — the subject (Lloyd's of London) is missing, likely a rendering artifact or truncation. This looks sloppy.
holes_found.recommendation: Fix to: 'Lloyd's of London does not charge premium rates for the region.'

stronger_arguments.description: The geometric symmetry claim is quantifiably wrong. The Bermuda Triangle (centered ~25°N, 71°W) and the Devil's Sea (centered ~25°N, 137°E) are separated by 208 degrees of longitude, NOT 180°. That's a 28-degree (15.6%) deviation from the claimed 'antipodal symmetry.' Even the agonic lines themselves aren't 180° apart — the western agonic line runs at roughly 80°W and the eastern at roughly 100-120°E, giving ~180-200° separation at most latitudes. The dome claim cherry-picks two famous regions that happen to be in different hemispheres and calls it 'geometric symmetry' without computing the actual angular separation.
sources: Larry Kusche, 'The Bermuda Triangle Mystery — Solved' (1975)

stronger_arguments.description: The agonic lines don't even pass through these regions currently. The western agonic line grazes only the western edge of the Bermuda Triangle (near Florida, ~80°W), while the eastern agonic line runs through the Malay Peninsula and Indonesia (~100-120°E), well west of the Devil's Sea at 137°E. Agonic line positions migrate due to secular variation of Earth's magnetic field — in the 1700s the western agonic did pass through the Bermuda area, but it has since moved. This migration is fully explained by geodynamo theory (standard model) and provides zero support for fixed dome geometry.
sources: Mandea & Korte (2011), Geomagnetic Observations and Models, Springer

stronger_arguments.description: The 'Devil's Sea' legend itself was debunked by Larry Kusche in 1975. The Japanese Maritime Safety Agency's actual warning zone was only 10 miles around Myōjin-shō, a volcanic seamount — not a vast paranormal region. Most 'lost ships' were small fishing vessels lost in known storm conditions. Charles Berlitz invented the parallel to the Bermuda Triangle in 1974; no Japanese sources previously identified the region as anomalous. So the dome model is treating two debunked maritime legends as data points.
sources: Larry Kusche, 'The Bermuda Triangle Mystery — Solved' (1975)
Wikipedia: Devil's Sea, citing Japanese Maritime Safety Agency records

kernel_of_truth: The dome model correctly identifies that agonic lines (zero magnetic declination) are a real and scientifically important geomagnetic feature. There are indeed two primary agonic lines on Earth's surface, and they do run through roughly opposite hemispheres. The concept that zero-declination corridors might have practical navigational significance is valid — historically, agonic lines were important for maritime navigation because compass bearings matched true bearings along them. However, this is entirely explained by the standard geodynamo model, and the lines are neither perfectly symmetric nor associated with anomalous disappearances.
deeper_analysis:
WIN-028 claims that the Bermuda Triangle and the Devil's Sea (near Japan) exhibit '180-degree longitudinal symmetry' along agonic lines, and that both are disappearance zones, validating dome geometry. This claim layers three distinct assertions, each of which fails independently.

First, the disappearance zone claim. Both the Bermuda Triangle and the Devil's Sea have been thoroughly debunked as regions of anomalous disappearances. Lloyd's of London confirmed they do not charge higher insurance premiums for the Bermuda Triangle. NOAA attributes losses to mundane environmental factors — hurricanes, Gulf Stream currents, and shallow waters. The U.S. Coast Guard found disappearance rates are 'relatively insignificant considering the number of ships and aircraft that pass through on a regular basis.' Larry Kusche's 1975 investigation found most Bermuda Triangle 'mysteries' involved inaccurate reporting, omitted weather data, or ships that were never actually lost. The Devil's Sea legend is even weaker — Kusche showed the Japanese Maritime Safety Agency's actual warning zone was only 10 miles around Myōjin-shō volcano, and Charles Berlitz essentially invented the parallel to sell books in 1974.

Second, the geometric symmetry claim. The dome asserts '~180° longitudinal symmetry,' but the actual separation between Bermuda Triangle (centered ~25°N, 71°W) and the Devil's Sea (~25°N, 137°E) is 208 degrees — a 28-degree deviation from the claimed 180°. This is a 15.6% error, which exceeds the tolerances the dome model applies to its own predictions in other domains. Even the agonic lines themselves aren't precisely 180° apart; the western agonic runs at roughly 80°W and the eastern through roughly 100-120°E at equatorial latitudes.

Third, the agonic line association. The western agonic line currently passes near the Florida coast (~80°W), grazing only the western edge of the conventionally defined Bermuda Triangle. The eastern agonic line runs through Southeast Asia and the Indian Ocean at roughly 100-120°E — approximately 17-37 degrees west of the Devil's Sea at 137°E. Neither agonic line passes through its respective 'disappearance zone.' Moreover, agonic lines migrate over decades due to secular variation of Earth's core field, a process fully modeled by the standard geodynamo. They are not fixed geometric features that would arise from a static dome cavity.

Our current rebuttal correctly cites NOAA, Lloyd's, and USCG to debunk the disappearance claim, but it misses the opportunity to demolish the geometric argument with simple coordinate arithmetic. Adding the 208° vs. 180° calculation and noting that agonic lines don't actually pass through these regions would make the rebuttal considerably stronger. The detail_evidence text also has a minor grammatical issue (missing subject 'Lloyd's of London') and uses a generic NOAA homepage link rather than a specific source. These are easy fixes that would improve credibility.

Notably, WIN-028 has NO code in the dome's repository. The monitor.py 39-domain validation engine does not include any domain for this WIN — no agonic line tracking, no geomagnetic symmetry calculation, no disappearance rate data. This is a pure assertion with zero computational backing, making it one of the weakest WINs in the entire model.
